title: Tunable Crossed Andreev Reflection in Bipolar Magnetic Semiconductors
abstract: Crossed Andreev reflection (CAR) is a nonlocal quantum transport phenomenon that arises at the interface between a superconductor and two spatially separated metals. In this process, an electron incident from one metal combines with another electron originating from the other metal to form a Cooper pair in the superconductor. As a consequence, a hole is emitted into the second metal, establishing a nonlocal electron-hole conversion process. In contrast to local Andreev reflection -- where electron-to-hole conversion occurs within the same region -- CAR intrinsically links two spatially separated carriers, giving rise to nonlocal correlations and quantum entanglement. In bipolar magnetic semiconductors (BMSs), the conduction and valence bands possess opposite spin polarizations. We propose to achieve tunable control of CAR by independently adjusting the chemical potentials of the two regions. By engineering the alignment of spin-polarized bands in the two BMS leads, CAR can be selectively enhanced or suppressed. This tunability enables precise manipulation of nonlocal transport, and correlated electron dynamics, offering promising prospects for spintronic and superconducting device applications.
summary: This theoretical work proposes using a heterostructure combining a superconductor with two Bipolar Magnetic Semiconductors (BMSs) to control quantum transport. By tuning the chemical potentials of the BMS leads to achieve opposite spin polarizations, the researchers demonstrate a purely electrical method to selectively enhance Crossed Andreev Reflection (CAR). This offers a promising platform for building spintronic and superconducting devices capable of manipulating nonlocal quantum correlations.
main_problem: The goal is to achieve tunable control over Crossed Andreev Reflection (CAR) in solid-state devices to manipulate nonlocal, spin-entangled electron pairs.
main_result: By independently tuning the chemical potentials of the two Bipolar Magnetic Semiconductor (BMS) leads to opposite signs, CAR can be selectively enhanced while suppressing competing processes like AR and ET.
method: The analysis uses the Landauer-Büttiker scattering formalism applied to a coupled BMS-SC-BMS heterostructure.
model_or_system: The system consists of a central s-wave superconductor (SC) coupled to two BMS leads. BMSs are characterized by spin-polarized conduction and valence bands, allowing for electrical control over spin alignment.
key_observables: Nonlocal conductivity ($	ext{G}_{	ext{RL}}$) and CAR transmission probability ($T_{	ext{CAR}}$), which are sensitive to the chemical potentials and SC length.
important_parameters: The chemical potentials ($\mu_{	ext{bl}}, \mu_{	ext{br}}$) are the primary tuning knobs, which can be set to opposite signs ($\mu_{	ext{bl}} = -\mu_{	ext{br}}$). The superconducting gap ($\Delta$) and SC length ($L$) are also critical parameters.
main_assumptions: The model assumes normal incidence for conductivity calculations and relies on the ability to tune the chemical potentials independently to control spin polarization.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the device schematic, dispersion relations, and show the dependence of CAR and ER probabilities on energy and chemical potential, as well as the oscillatory behavior of conductivities versus SC length.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces CAR and the BMS system, then develops the Hamiltonian and scattering formalism. It details the tunability mechanism by setting opposite chemical potentials, leading to the calculation of enhanced, controllable nonlocal transport phenomena.
why_interesting: The ability to use purely electrostatic means (tuning chemical potentials) to control superconducting correlations and spin-entangled transport is highly relevant for building quantum devices that interact with superconducting qubits or quantum memories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Dispersion relations of the left and right BMS for µbl = −µbr = 4b. The red (blue) lines represent the up-spin (down-spin) electron channels, while the dotted lines corre- spond to the respective hole channels.

FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the proposed device geom- etry, comprising a central s-wave superconductor sandwiched between two bipolar magnetic semiconductor leads. Indepen- dent, tunable gate voltages applied to each BMS electrode provide local control over the chemical potential. By tuning the respective Fermi energies such that the charge carriers participating in transport in the left and right leads possess opposite spin polarizations, crossed Andreev reflection is sig- nificantly enhanced while competing electron tunneling is nat- urally suppressed.
